text: My name is Joel Dunning and this brilliant operation has been done by Alexander Kern, a senior consultant in Coswig, the Department of Thoracic Surgery in Dresden.
So I'll take you through it.
Just in the initial view, he's put 10 millimetres of mercury pressure in.

He's got an artisensual bipolar instrument and he was just checking for the tension on the diaphragm and then he's changed over to the artisensual needle holder and a fenestrated grasper in his left hand.
You saw a nice picture of the port placement.
He has his endoscopic port just behind the scapula.
The left-sided port is just anterior to the scapula at about the fifth space.
And then the posterior port should be put in under vision and of note just should be not too high so that you're not pointing down too much because the CO2 is really key
for pushing the diaphragm down so that you can get a really good tight diaphragm placation.
So, Alexander is doing horizontal, plagiated mattress sutures, as you can see here.
And it's a really good, nice suture.
And then here you can see the Tie Knot device.
This is a really great idea.
It saves endoscopic tying of knots.

also the diaphragm can really be under quite a lot of tension so it can be difficult to tie knots in the chest he's now looking to see how tense he should do the next suture of note he does from lateral to medial and the advantage of that is that the diaphragm is under the least tension laterally so as you now come medially and the liver is pushing up really hard it is easier to do these subsequent stitches
So he's going to proceed to do probably about 8 to 10 horizontal mattress sutures, every single one pledgeted.
Nice and easy to bring the pledges into the chest.
As you can see, you can put them in through the port.
And here's the second tie knot, which is a great device to do knot tying.
It's very safe.
There's several on the market, tie knot, core knot, and certainly highly recommended.

he's using a zero polyester non-absorbable suture obviously you should not use absorbable sutures in these cases and the bigger the suture the better really really the limitation to size of sutures is only the size of the needle can you get the needle down your your ports I usually use a 10 millimeter port anteriorly so that I can get the pledges in really nicely and also so you can get the needles in and I think Alexander does that also

just a note on Alexander Kern he's an absolutely fantastic endoscopic surgeon he's already done 60 artescential wristed instrument operations including five diaphragm placations but he's done lobectomies segmentectomies thymectomies so a real wealth of experience with this

fantastic range of wristed instruments as you can see by the initial slide we showed right at the start of this video there are at least 11 different instruments available from Art Essential as well as the grasper you see here there are there are a variety of different graspers and there's actually a precise needle holder which is a smaller needle holder not appropriate for these big sutures but is available if you're going to maybe do a

um a bronchoplasty for example by vats also artist central is a very far-sighted company they're creating devices all the time and there's a lot in the pipeline so look out for smaller devices less than eight millimeter and also look for advanced energy which is coming very soon from their range

if we look into the operation just right now you can see he's doing a great job of doing several stitches to placate and bunch up the diaphragm also he grasps nicely with his left hand so there's no chance of getting some bowel or liver below the diaphragm because you know you've got this beautiful ruffle

to stitch through very safely so I've also done about 70 or 80 diaphragm placations and I've yet to cause any damage under the diaphragm because you use that technique of bunching up the diaphragm with your left hand and stitching just next to your needle holder so here you can see he's just swapping hands just to make sure he gets the right depth and the right tightness
And you can see with his right hand now, he's bunched that diaphragm.
So if he stitches through that, that will be perfect and very, very safe.
Also, you can see him stitching through the tenderness portion, which is very strong.
And then sometimes the muscular portion is less strong, which is why the pledges are very important.

I've noticed sometimes you get a little bit of bleeding because it's quite a vascular structure the diaphragm don't worry about it at all because when you tie the knot of the suture that tends to stop the bleeding or occasionally a single extra suture now I do recommend and I'm sure Alex will agree that we try and do as tight as possible to get this diaphragm down as possible and here's just a little example of a tiny bit of bleeding which you know is not going to be important we'll just tie the knot and we'll be fine
I'll just mention a little bit about the patient.
This patient is a 56 year old male and he's had a paralysed diaphragm for four years after an accident and he's got a really pronounced restrictive ventilatory defect.
His breathing is worse on bending forwards trying to tie his shoelaces or lying back.

and of note these patients also are very short of breath if they try swimming all manoeuvres that elevate the diaphragm into the chest he had a fluoroscopy and you can do a fluoroscopy or a sniff test to prove paralysis and we also get a CT scan just to make sure that there isn't something like a tumour affecting the phrenic trying to find a cause for the diagnosis but mostly it's due to post-viral or due to injury

Another note on this patient is that after this diaphragm placation we place a drain and Alex puts a 24 drain in and this drain was removed in this patient on day two and then it was removed day three and he went home day three with very little pain which is a massive advantage of the endoscopic technique over any open technique.

The other massive advantage you can see here is the diaphragm is pushed down with the CO2 at 10 millimetres of mercury pressure so actually I think this is one of the few operations that is actually easier by vats than open because open the diaphragm is pushed right up into the field of view whereas the CO2 does a great job of making it a nice flat surface to operate on.
We're now beyond halfway doing our horizontal mattress sutures and you can really see it's a really nice tight diaphragm placation.
Of note, you want to get each suture next to each other and all the strength comes in the distance you travel between the two horizontal mattress sutures.
So keep them fairly wide and then put all your sutures fairly next to each other for maximum strength.
In particularly strong individuals, we might over-sew some of these or we might even put a mesh over the top for added strength.
But usually a nice line of horizontal mastris sutures are the most important.
It's looking really nice now.
You can see that this is much better than preoperatively.

just getting one extra suture just to really push down that central portion of note the central portion is the bit under the most tension and therefore it's a really good idea to buttress this with a second suture perhaps just as Alex has done here
So all in all, I think you'll agree that Alex has done an absolutely brilliant job.
Really minimally invasive, a five mil port and two 10 mil ports.
There's no utility incision because nothing's coming out.
So it's much better for pain.
You can get these people up and about really quickly.
And it was great to see this patient going home on day three.
really well with a significant improvement immediately of his breathing.
These patients go home less short of breath than they come in, which is really satisfying.
So well done, Alex.
